rubric_items.reasoning: The fix is a correct, root-cause configuration change: enable-opentelemetry=true, otlp-collector-host/port pointing at otel-collector.otel.svc:4317, otel-sampler=AlwaysOn with ratio 1.0, and log-format-upstream embedding $opentelemetry_trace_id/$opentelemetry_span_id — all applied via the ingress-nginx-controller ConfigMap, which is the officially supported mechanism and is declarative/durable across restarts (nginx.conf shows 'opentelemetry on;' baked into the generated server blocks, and opentelemetry.toml correctly reflects AlwaysOn/ratio=1 pointing at the right collector). The agent didn't stop at the log line looking populated — it independently queried the otel-collector's own logs and confirmed the exact same trace_id/span_id plus matching http.target and http.server_name arrived server-side, which is strong evidence of a genuine, working pipeline rather than a coincidental pass.

The visible portion of the log shows a clean, logical verification sequence: confirm the opentelemetry.toml on the running pod reflects the intended sampler/exporter settings, send the exact test request specified in the task, tail the controller access log to confirm non-empty trace/span IDs, then cross-check the same IDs against the collector's own logs for end-to-end confirmation. No flailing, no dead ends, no unnecessary retries visible. Slight uncertainty only because the log head (where the actual ConfigMap edit and initial diagnosis happened) was truncated and not fully visible.

All commands are targeted and least-privilege: kubectl exec to cat a specific config file, the exact curl test request given in the task spec, kubectl logs --tail on the controller and collector, and a scoped grep to isolate the relevant trace. No broad deletes, no unscoped mutations, no unnecessary privilege use — well matched to a config-verification task.

Only 1 mutation across 37 K8s API calls (the single ConfigMap update), with the rest being reads for verification. This is the minimal footprint needed to make the change and confirm it — no collateral resources touched, no restarts or redeploys, ingress-nginx picked up the change via its native reload mechanism.
